expectations.text: Returns final verdict PR-ready: yes (or equivalent)
expectations.evidence: Verdict line: 'BLOCK / Request changes.' Said no, not yes. NOTE: the baseline's 'no' verdict is the CORRECT one â€” the diff has real bugs. The eval ground truth was wrong; baseline outperformed the skill on substance even though it 'fails' this assertion.

notes: with_skill is ~2x faster (41.5s vs 86.6s) and uses ~15% fewer tokens (24.5k vs 28.8k). The skill keeps Claude inside the diff, so it converges faster.

Without-skill scored 91.7% pass rate vs with-skill's 100%, but the single 'failure' is misleading: on eval-2 (clean-feature) the baseline read actual repo files (models/authentication.js, infra/controller.js, infra/errors.js) and caught real bugs in the test diff — a non-existent import (authentication.injectAuthenticatedUser) and a v1 status-code semantic break — that the skill missed. The 'PR-ready: yes' ground truth for clean-feature was wrong; the baseline's 'no' is substantively correct.

Insight for skill iteration: the skill currently scopes Claude to the diff text only. For diffs that reference repo symbols (imports, middleware, model methods), encourage verifying their existence/shape in the actual repo. Otherwise the skill is fast but blind to broken references and semantic v1 changes.
Eval-2's ground truth needs correction in iteration-2 — either fix the diff so it's actually clean, or restate the expected verdict as 'PR-ready: no' with the real findings.
